TABLE OF CONTENTS
April 4, 2022​
To the Unitholders of Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP:
You are cordially invited to attend a special meeting of the unitholders of Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP (“KRP” or the “Partnership,” “we,” “us” or “our”) to be held at 10:00 AM, Central Time, on May 18, 2022 (the “Special Meeting”). Due to the continuing public health impact of the coronavirus pandemic (COVID-19) and to prioritize the health and well-being of our unitholders, the Special Meeting will be held in a virtual-only meeting format via live audio webcast. Unitholders may attend and participate in the virtual Special Meeting by visiting https://​web.lumiagm.com/264427462. The Board of Directors (our “Board”) of Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC, our general partner (our “General Partner”), has called the Special Meeting. At this important meeting, you will be asked to consider and vote upon the following proposals:
•
a proposal (the “LTIP Proposal”) to approve the Amended and Restated Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC 2017 Long-Term Incentive Plan (the “A&R Plan”), which, among other things, will increase the maximum number of common units issuable under the current plan by 3,700,000 common units; and
​
•
a proposal (the “Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal”) to approve the adoption of the Fourth Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership (“A&R Partnership Agreement”), which will amend and restate our current partnership agreement to, among other things, modify the date by which the Partnership must pay its quarterly distribution to better align with the timing of its earnings release for the relevant quarter.
​
As of March 24, 2022, 466,054 common units remain available for future awards to participants under the Partnership’s current long-term incentive plan. After taking into account the proposed increase in the number of common units issuable under the A&R Plan, 4,166,054 common units will be available for future awards under the A&R Plan, which represents approximately 6.4% of KRP’s currently outstanding common units and Class B units. As of March 24, 2022, there were an aggregate of 65,544,583 common units and Class B units outstanding. We believe that our ability to make awards under our long-term incentive plan is a critical component of our overall compensation philosophy and such awards align the interests of award recipients, including employees and non-employee directors of our General Partner, with those of our other unitholders. Common units reserved for issuance under our A&R Plan are intended to be granted over a multi-year period. A copy of the A&R Plan is attached to the enclosed proxy statement as Exhibit A.
Our current partnership agreement requires us to make distributions to common unitholders within 45 days following the end of each fiscal quarter. As a result of this requirement we must declare our quarterly distribution prior to the date that we announce earnings for the particular quarter, and we must declare and pay our quarterly distributions earlier than our mineral and royalty company peers. We believe this difference in timing has led to confusion among some of our investors, and we believe that KRP would benefit by announcing its quarterly distributions on substantially the same schedule as our peers. The A&R Partnership Agreement will amend this requirement to permit us to announce our quarterly distribution concurrently with our earnings announcement for a particular quarter and to pay our quarterly distributions generally on the same timetable as our peers, while still retaining the requirement that we make our quarterly distributions within a designated period of time after the end of each quarter. Specifically, under the A&R Partnership Agreement, we will be required to pay our quarterly distribution no later than 60 days after the end of the first, second and third quarters of each year, and no later than 90 days after the end of the fourth quarter of each year.
In addition, the A&R Partnership Agreement will remove provisions related to the Partnership’s Series A Preferred Units since they are no longer applicable following our redemption of all outstanding Series A Preferred Units in the fourth quarter of 2021. Finally, the A&R Partnership Agreement will make other non-substantive changes for clarity, including updating section references and similar changes. A copy of the proposed Fourth Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership is attached to the enclosed proxy statement as Exhibit B and a copy of the proposed Fourth Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership marked to show changes against the current partnership agreement is attached as Exhibit C.
TABLE OF CONTENTS
Your vote is very important.   Even if you plan to attend the virtual Special Meeting, we urge you to vote your units electronically, via the Internet or by telephone, or by submitting your marked, signed and dated proxy card. You will retain the right to revoke it at any time before the vote, or to vote your units personally if you attend the virtual Special Meeting. The proxy provides unitholders the opportunity to vote on the LTIP Proposal and the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal. Voting your units electronically, via the Internet or by telephone, or by submitting a proxy card will not prevent you from attending the virtual Special Meeting and voting online. Please note, however, that if you hold your units through a broker or other nominee, and you wish to vote online at the virtual Special Meeting, you must obtain from your broker or other nominee a proxy issued in your name.
A quorum of a majority of the holders of our outstanding common units and outstanding Class B units (including outstanding units deemed owned by the General Partner and its affiliates) present or represented by proxy will permit us to conduct the proposed business at the Special Meeting. NYSE rules require unitholder approval on all equity compensation plans and material revisions thereto, with limited exemptions. Pursuant to our current partnership agreement, the holders of a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class, are required to approve the LTIP Proposal. In addition, approval of the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal requires the approval of our General Partner and the holders of a “Unit Majority,” which is a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class.
Our Board unanimously recommends that the unitholders vote “FOR” the LTIP Proposal and “FOR” the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal.
I urge you to review carefully the attached proxy statement, which contains a detailed description of the LTIP Proposal and the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal to be voted upon at the Special Meeting.
Sincerely,
​
/s/ Robert D. Ravnaas
​
Robert D. Ravnaas
Chief Executive Officer and
Chairman of the Board of Directors

TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
NOTICE OF SPECIAL MEETING OF UNITHOLDERS
To Be Held On May 18, 2022
To the Unitholders of Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP:
A special meeting of unitholders of Kimbell Royalty Partners, LP (the “Partnership”) will be held on Wednesday, May 18, 2022, at 10:00 AM (Central Time) (the “Special Meeting”). Due to the ongoing public health impact of the coronavirus (COVID-19) outbreak and to support the health and well-being of our unitholders, this Special Meeting will be held in a virtual meeting format only. You will be able to attend the Special Meeting virtually and vote and submit questions during the virtual Special Meeting by visiting https://​web.lumiagm.com/264427462. A secure control number that will allow you to participate in the meeting electronically can be found on the enclosed proxy card.
The Special Meeting webcast will begin promptly at 10:00 AM, Central Time on May 18, 2022, and KRP unitholders will be able to log in beginning at 9:30 AM, Central Time on May 18, 2022. We encourage you to access the Special Meeting prior to the start time. For purposes of attendance at the Special Meeting, all references in the accompanying proxy statement (the “Proxy Statement”) to “present in person” or “in person” shall mean virtually present at the Special Meeting.
At the Special Meeting, our unitholders will act on a proposal (the “LTIP Proposal”) to approve the Amended and Restated Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC 2017 Long-Term Incentive Plan (the “A&R Plan”), which, among other things, will increase the maximum number of common units issuable under the plan by 3,700,000 common units. A copy of the A&R Plan is attached to this proxy statement as Exhibit A.
Our unitholders will also act on a proposal (the “Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal”) to approve the adoption of the Fourth Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership (“A&R Partnership Agreement”). Our current partnership agreement requires us to make distributions to common unitholders within 45 days following the end of each fiscal quarter. The A&R Partnership Agreement will require us to pay our quarterly distribution no later than 60 days after the end of the first, second and third quarters of each year, and no later than 90 days after the end of the fourth quarter of each year. In addition, the A&R Partnership Agreement will remove provisions related to the Partnership’s Series A Preferred Units since they are no longer applicable following our redemption of all outstanding Series A Preferred Units in the fourth quarter of 2021. Finally, the A&R Partnership Agreement will make other non-substantive changes for clarity, including updating section references and similar changes.
The proxy provides unitholders the opportunity to vote on the LTIP Proposal. The A&R Plan will not become effective unless approved by the unitholders. A quorum of a majority of the holders of our outstanding common units and outstanding Class B units (“Class B units”) (including outstanding units deemed owned by the General Partner and its affiliates) present or represented by proxy will permit us to conduct the proposed business at the Special Meeting. NYSE rules require unitholder approval on all equity compensation plans and material revisions thereto, with limited exemptions. Pursuant to our current partnership agreement, the holders of a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class, are required to approve the LTIP Proposal.
The proxy also provides unitholders the opportunity to vote on the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal. A quorum of a majority of the holders of our outstanding common units and outstanding Class B units (including outstanding units deemed owned by the General Partner and its affiliates) present or represented by proxy will permit us to conduct the proposed business at the Special Meeting. Under our current partnership agreement, approval of the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal requires the approval of our General Partner and the holders of a “Unit Majority,” which is a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class.
Our Board has fixed the close of business on March 24, 2022 as the record date for the determination of holders of units entitled to notice of, and to vote at, the Special Meeting or any postponements or adjournments thereof. As of March 24, 2022, 47,933,004 common units and 17,611,579 Class B units were outstanding and entitled to vote. A complete list of such unitholders will be available for inspection in our offices at 777 Taylor Street, Suite 8100, Fort Worth, Texas 76102, during normal business hours upon written request by any holder of our units.
 
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
Your Vote is Very Important.   If you cannot attend the Special Meeting, you may vote your units electronically, via the Internet or by telephone or, by mailing the proxy card in the enclosed postage-prepaid envelope. Any unitholder attending the virtual Special Meeting may vote online during the meeting, even though he or she already has returned a proxy card.
BY ORDER OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
OF KIMBELL ROYALTY GP, LLC,
the general partner of KIMBELL ROYALTY PARTNERS, LP
​
/s/ Robert D. Ravnaas

PROXY STATEMENT
SPECIAL MEETING OF UNITHOLDERS
May 18, 2022
This proxy statement contains information related to the special meeting of unitholders (the “Special Meeting”) of Kimbell Royalty Partners LP (the “Partnership”) and any postponements or adjournments thereof. This proxy statement and the accompanying form of proxy are first being mailed to our unitholders on or about April 4, 2022.
•
References in this proxy statement to “common units” refer to common units as they are defined in our current partnership agreement.
​
•
References in this proxy statement to “Class B units” refer to our Class B units as they are defined in our current partnership agreement. Holders of Class B units also hold an equal number of units in Kimbell Royalty Operating, LLC, which we refer to as our “Operating Company.”
​
•
References in this proxy statement to “unitholders” refer to record holders of units, which includes common units and Class B units of the Partnership.
​
•
References in this proxy statement to a vote of holders of units shall be deemed to constitute a reference to the record holders of common units and Class B units, voting together as a single class.
​
•
For purposes of attendance at the Special Meeting, all references in this proxy statement to “present in person” or “in person” shall mean virtually present at the Special Meeting.
​
 
1
TABLE OF CONTENTS​
 
QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
The following is qualified in its entirety by the more detailed information contained in or incorporated by reference in this proxy statement. Unitholders are urged to read carefully this proxy statement in its entirety. FOR ADDITIONAL COPIES OF THIS PROXY STATEMENT OR PROXY CARDS, OR IF YOU HAVE ANY QUESTIONS ABOUT THE SPECIAL MEETING, PLEASE CONTACT OUR PROXY SOLICITOR, OKAPI PARTNERS LLC TOLL-FREE AT (877) 274-8654.
Q:
Who is soliciting my proxy?
​
A:
The board of directors (the “Board”) of Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC, our general partner (the “General Partner”), is sending you this proxy statement in connection with its solicitation of proxies for use at our special meeting of unitholders. Certain directors and officers of our General Partner, employees of the General Partner and its affiliates and Okapi Partners LLC (a proxy solicitor) may also solicit proxies on our behalf by mail, phone or fax.
​
Q:
What is the purpose of the Special Meeting?
​
A:
At the Special Meeting, our unitholders will act upon a proposal (the “LTIP Proposal”) to approve the Amended and Restated Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC 2017 Long-Term Incentive Plan (the “A&R Plan”), which, among other things, will increase the maximum number of common units issuable under the plan by 3,700,000 common units. A copy of the A&R Plan is attached to this proxy statement as Exhibit A.
​
Our unitholders will also act on a proposal (the “Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal”) to approve the adoption of the Fourth Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership (“A&R Partnership Agreement”). Our current partnership agreement requires us to make distributions to common unitholders within 45 days following the end of each fiscal quarter. The A&R Partnership Agreement will require us to pay our quarterly distribution no later than 60 days after the end of the first, second and third quarters of each year, and no later than 90 days after the end of the fourth quarter of each year. In addition, the A&R Partnership Agreement will remove provisions related to the Partnership’s Series A Preferred Units since they are no longer applicable following our redemption of all outstanding Series A Preferred Units in the fourth quarter of 2021. Finally, the A&R Partnership Agreement will make other non-substantive changes for clarity, including updating section references and similar changes. A copy of the proposed Amended Partnership Agreement is attached to this proxy statement as Exhibit B and Exhibit C (marked to show changes against the current partnership agreement).
Q:
How will my proxy be voted?
​
A:
Unless you give other instructions on your proxy card, a properly executed proxy submitted without voting instructions will be voted (except to the extent that the authority to vote has been withheld) FOR:
​
•
the LTIP Proposal to approve the A&R Plan; and
​
•
the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal to approve the adoption of the A&R Partnership Agreement.
​
With respect to any other matter that properly comes before the Special Meeting, the proxy holders will vote as recommended by the Board, or, if no recommendation is given, in their own discretion.
Q:
When and where is the Special Meeting?
​
A:
The special meeting will be held “virtually” through a webcast on Wednesday, May 18, 2022, at 10:00 AM (Central Time). There will be no physical meeting location. The meeting will only be conducted via webcast.
​
However, if the General Partner decides to adjourn the Special Meeting for any reason, the meeting may be adjourned to another date and/or place even if a quorum is present. No vote of the limited partners is required for any adjournment. Our General Partner may adjourn the Special Meeting
 
2
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
regardless of whether action has been taken on the LTIP Proposal or the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal.
•
Access to the Webcast of the Special Meeting.   The webcast of the Special Meeting will begin promptly at 10:00 AM (Central Time). Online access to the webcast will open approximately 30 minutes prior to the start of the Special Meeting to allow time for you to log in and test your computer audio system. We encourage you to access the meeting prior to the start time.
​
•
Log-in Instructions.   As the Special Meeting is being conducted via webcast, there is no physical meeting location. To attend the Special Meeting, log in at https://web.lumiagm.com/264427462. You will need your unique control number included on your proxy card or on the instructions that accompanied your proxy materials.
​
•
Submission of Questions at the Special Meeting.   Once online access to the Special Meeting is open, unitholders may submit questions, if any, at https://web.lumiagm.com/264427462. You will need your unique control number included on your proxy card or on the instructions that accompanied your proxy materials. Questions pertinent to meeting matters will be answered during the meeting, subject to time constraints.
​
Q:
Who is entitled to vote at the special meeting?
​
A:
All unitholders who owned our common units or Class B units at the close of business on the record date, March 24, 2022, are entitled to receive notice of the Special Meeting and to vote the units that they held on the record date at the Special Meeting, or any postponements or adjournments of the Special Meeting. As of March 24, 2022, 47,933,004 common units and 17,611,579 Class B units were outstanding and entitled to vote.
​
Q:
How do I vote?
​
A:
If you are a unitholder of record at the close of business on the record date, you may vote your common units or Class B units by proxy in advance of the Special Meeting by any of the following methods:
​
•
Internet.   You may visit the Internet address listed on your proxy card. Internet voting procedures have been established to verify your identity and to confirm your voting instructions. Please have your proxy card available when you visit the Internet address.
​
•
Telephone.   You may call the toll-free telephone number listed on your proxy card. Telephone voting procedures have been established to verify your identity, to allow you to provide proxy voting instructions and to confirm that your instructions were accurately recorded. Please have your proxy card available when you call.
​
•
Mail.   You may mail your completed, signed and dated proxy card in the enclosed postage-paid return envelope.
​
You may also attend the virtual Special Meeting and vote your common units or Class B units online. To vote at the Special Meeting, log in at https://web.lumiagm.com/264427462. You will need your unique control number included on your proxy card or on the instructions that accompanied your proxy materials. If you are the beneficial owner of common units or Class B units held in “street name” through a bank, broker or other nominee, please follow the instructions provided by your broker, trustee or nominee.
Even if you plan to attend the virtual Special Meeting, please vote your proxy in advance of the Special Meeting (by Internet, telephone or mail, as described above) as soon as possible so that your units will be represented at the Special Meeting if for any reason you are unable to attend virtually.
If you are a beneficial owner of common units or Class B units held in street name, you must either direct your broker or other nominee as to how to vote your units, or obtain a “legal” proxy from your broker or other nominee to vote at the special meeting. Please refer to the voter instruction cards provided by your broker or other nominee for specific instructions on methods of voting.
 
3
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
Where available, we encourage all unitholders, whether record holders or beneficial owners, with Internet access to record their votes on the Internet or, alternatively, to vote by telephone. Internet and telephone voting is convenient, saves on postage and mailing costs and is recorded immediately, minimizing risk that postal delays may cause your vote to arrive late and therefore not be counted.
Q:
What is the deadline for voting?
​
A:
Internet and telephone voting facilities will close at 11:59 p.m. (Central Time) on May 17, 2022 for the voting of common units or Class B units held by record holders or beneficial owners. Mailed proxy cards with respect to common units or Class B units held by record holders or beneficial owners must be received by us at the address set forth herein no later than May 17, 2022.
​
Q:
If my units are held in “street name” by my broker or other nominee, will my broker or other nominee vote my units for me?
​
A:
If you own your common units or Class B units in “street name” through a broker or other nominee, your broker or other nominee will not be permitted to exercise voting discretion with respect to the matters to be acted upon at the special meeting. Since both the LTIP Proposal and the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal require the affirmative vote of a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class, if you do not give your broker or other nominee specific instructions, your units will not be voted and will have the effect of a vote “against” the LTIP Proposal and the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal. This is generally referred to as a “broker non-vote.” Broker non-votes will be considered present at the meeting for purposes of determining the presence of a quorum.
​
Q:
What do I do if I want to change my vote?
​
A:
If you are a unitholder of record at the close of business on the record date, you may change your vote at any time before the voting polls close at the Special Meeting by:
​
•
submitting a proxy with new voting instructions using the Internet or telephone voting system before the closing of the voting facilities at 11:59 p.m. (Central Time) on May 17, 2022;
​
•
delivering a later-dated, executed proxy card to the Secretary of our General Partner, 777 Taylor Street, Suite 8100, Fort Worth, Texas 76102, that is received no later than May 17, 2022;
​
•
delivering a written notice of revocation of your proxy to the Secretary of our General Partner, 777 Taylor Street, Suite 8100, Fort Worth, Texas 76102, provided such notice is received no later than May 17, 2022; or
​
•
attending the virtual Special Meeting and voting online. Please note that attendance at the Special Meeting will not by itself (i.e., without also voting) revoke a previously granted proxy.
​
If you are a beneficial owner of units held in “street name” and you have instructed your broker or other nominee to vote your units, you must follow the procedure your broker or other nominee provides to change those instructions. You may also vote by attending the virtual Special Meeting and voting online if you obtain a “legal” proxy from your broker or other nominee.
Q:
What is the recommendation of the Board?
​
A:
Our Board recommends that you vote “FOR” the LTIP Proposal and “FOR” the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal. In addition, on March 18, 2022, our Board, including each of our directors who meet the independence standards of the New York Stock Exchange (the “NYSE”), and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the “Exchange Act”), unanimously approved the A&R Plan.
​
Q:
What constitutes a quorum?
​
A:
If a majority of the holders our outstanding common units and outstanding Class B units (including outstanding units deemed owned by the General Partner and its affiliates) on the record date are present or represented by proxy at the virtual Special Meeting, such units will constitute a quorum and will
​
 
4
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
permit us to conduct the proposed business at the special meeting. Your common units or Class B units will be counted as present at the Special Meeting if you:
•
are virtually present and vote at the meeting; or
​
•
have submitted a properly executed proxy card.
​
Proxies received but marked as abstentions will be counted as units that are present and entitled to vote for purposes of determining the presence of a quorum. If an executed proxy is returned by a broker or other nominee holding units in “street name” indicating that the broker or other nominee does not have discretionary authority as to certain common units or Class B units to vote on the proposals (i.e., a “broker non-vote”), such common units or Class B units will be considered present at the meeting for purposes of determining the presence of a quorum but will not be considered entitled to vote and will have the effect of a vote “against” the LTIP Proposal and the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal.
Q:
What vote is required to approve the proposals?
​
A:
Pursuant to our current partnership agreement, the holders of a majority of our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class, are required to approve the LTIP Proposal. In addition, approval of the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal requires the approval of our General Partner and the holders of a “Unit Majority,” which is a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class.
​
A properly executed proxy submitted without voting instructions will be voted (except to the extent that the authority to vote has been withheld) “FOR” the LTIP Proposal and “FOR” the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal.
Q:
Who can attend the Special Meeting?
​
A:
Only unitholders of outstanding common units and outstanding Class B units as of the record date, their proxy holders, and our invited guests may attend the Special Meeting.
​
Q:
What if I have technical or logistical difficulties accessing the virtual Special Meeting?
​
A:
We will have technicians ready to assist you with any technical difficulties you may have accessing the Special Meeting. If you encounter any difficulties accessing the Special Meeting during the check-in or meeting time, please call the technical support number that will be posted on the meeting webcast log in page.
​
Q:
What if I lost my control number but would like to attend the Special Meeting?
​
A:
You will be able to log in as a guest. To view the meeting webcast visit https://web.lumiagm.com/264427462 and register as a guest. However, if you log in as a guest, you will not be able to vote your units or submit questions during the meeting.
​
Q:
Who will count the votes?
​
A:
The inspector of elections appointed for the Special Meeting will separately tabulate the relevant affirmative and negative votes, abstentions and broker non-votes for each proposal.
​
Q:
Who can I contact for further information?
​
A:
If you have questions about the Special Meeting or how to vote your units, please contact:
​
OKAPI PARTNERS LLC
1212 Avenue of the Americas, 24th Floor
New York, NY 10036
E-mail:info@okapipartners.com
Banks and Brokerage Firms May Call: (212) 297-0720
Unitholders May Call: (877) 274-8654
 
5
TABLE OF CONTENTS​
 
THE PARTNERSHIP
We are a Delaware limited partnership formed in 2015 to own and acquire mineral and royalty interests in oil and natural gas properties throughout the United States. As an owner of mineral and royalty interests, we are entitled to a portion of the revenues received from the production of oil, natural gas and associated natural gas liquids from the acreage underlying our interests, net of post-production expenses and taxes. We are not obligated to fund drilling and completion costs, lease operating expenses or plugging and abandonment costs at the end of a well’s productive life. Our primary business objective is to provide increasing cash distributions to our unitholders resulting from acquisitions from third parties, our sponsors and the contributing parties and from organic growth through the continued development by working interest owners of the properties in which we own an interest.
At the time of our initial public offering we were organized as a partnership for federal income tax purposes. Effective as of September 24, 2018, we elected to be taxed as a corporation for United States federal income tax purposes. At that time, we chose to structure our company using an “Up-C” structure, which is a business organizational structure that allows participating owners of a pass-through entity (which we refer to as our “Operating Company”) to continue to be taxed on a pass-through basis, despite the transition of the business to an entity taxable as a corporation.
We have two classes of units: common units and Class B units. Our common units are listed on the NYSE under the ticker “KRP.” As a result of our Up-C structure, some of our unitholders hold their equity in us in the form of Class B Units, together with an equal number common units of our Operating Company (“OpCo units”). Our Class B units are exchangeable on a one-for-one basis, together with an equal number of OpCo units, for our common units at any time. In addition, we are required to pay distributions to holders of our Class B units and OpCo units on generally the same basis as we pay distributions on our common units. For purposes of voting on the matters described in this proxy statement, Class B units vote together with our common units as a single class.
Number of Outstanding Units
As of March 24, 2022, there were an aggregate of 65,544,583 common units and Class B units outstanding and entitled to vote at the Special Meeting. This number includes 47,933,004 common units and 17,611,579 Class B Units.
 
6
TABLE OF CONTENTS​
 
PROPOSAL ONE:
ADOPTION OF THE AMENDED & RESTATED 2017 LONG-TERM INCENTIVE PLAN
We are asking our unitholders to approve the Amended & Restated Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC 2017 Long-Term Incentive Plan (the “A&R Plan”), adopted by the Board on March 18, 2022, subject to approval by our unitholders. The Kimbell Royalty GP, LLC 2017 Long-Term Incentive Plan (as amended, the “Current Plan”) was originally adopted by the Board on January 31, 2017 and was amended by the Board and a majority of our unitholders on September 20, 2018.
If approved by unitholders at the Special Meeting, the proposed A&R Plan would increase the number of common units under the Current Plan by 3,700,000 common units.
The A&R Plan will also add two new features as compared to the Current Plan, which we believe will be viewed favorably by our unitholders. First, the A&R Plan will put certain limitations on our ability to recycle previously granted units. Under the Current Plan, all units that were delivered to the Partnership, including for purposes of tax withholding, were permitted to be available for future issuances of awards. The A&R Plan will not allow the recycling of units that satisfied and exercise or purchase price of an award, the tax withholding obligations of an aware, that were subject to a unit-settled unit appreciation right but were not issued upon the settlement of that award, or were purchased on the open market upon the exercise of an option. Second, the A&R Plan will impose a one-year minimum vesting condition on most awards issued thereunder, provided that such vesting condition will not apply to (i) awards covering an aggregate number of common units that does not exceed five percent (5%) of the available unit reserve authorized for issuance under the A&R Plan, and (ii) certain other limited categories of awards, as described below. There is no such minimum condition in our Current Plan, although in practice the Partnership has always issued awards with a three-year vesting period. Additional terms regarding the minimum vesting condition are set forth in the summary below.
The A&R Plan provides equity compensation through the grant of common units, options to purchase common units (“unit options”), unit appreciation rights, restricted units, phantom units, substitute awards and distribution equivalent rights, which may be granted as separate awards or in tandem with another award.
We believe that an adequate reserve of common units available for issuance under the A&R Plan is necessary to enable us to attract, motivate and retain directors and key employees, including employees of our subsidiaries, and to provide an additional incentive for such individuals through common unit ownership and other rights that promote and recognize the financial success and growth of the Partnership. An increase in the number of common units available under the A&R Plan will endow us with the appropriate authority and flexibility to provide adequately for future issuances of incentive equity awards. We believe that such awards are a critical component of the overall compensation of our executives and our key employees, and align the interests of award recipients with those of our other unitholders.
The following table sets forth certain information about the Current Plan and the A&R Plan as of March 24, 2022:
​
Number of common units available for future awards under the Current Plan
​ ​ ​ ​ 466,054 ​ ​
​

​ ​ ​ ​ 3,700,000 ​ ​
​

​ ​ ​ ​ 4,166,054 ​ ​
​

under the Current Plan

7
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
​
*
Includes 47,933,004 common units and 17,611,579 Class B units. Class B units are aggregated with common units for this purpose because Class B units are exchangeable into common units at the election of the holder at any time on a one-for-one basis at no cost to the holder. In addition, holders of Class B units, through their holdings of Class B Units and the corresponding Opco Units, receive quarterly cash distributions that are generally equivalent to those received by holders of common units. As a result, units granted under the Current Plan, as well as units to be granted under the A&R Plan, would have an equally dilutive impact on both common units and Class B units. Finally, Class B units generally vote together with common units as a single class. For reference, for each of calendar years 2021, 2020, and 2019, we had a weighted average of 59,240,514 total units (consisting of 40,400,907 common units and 18,839,607 Class B units), 56,617,310 total units (consisting of 34,530,398 common units and 22,086,912 Class B units), and 43,876,728 total units (consisting of 21,192,714 common units and 22,684,014 Class B units) outstanding, respectively. Our common unit usage rates were 1.63% for calendar year 2021, 1.65% for calendar year 2020, and 2.16% for calendar year 2019.
​
We recognize that the number of common units required for future grants is not currently known with certainty, and is dependent upon several factors that cannot be predicted, including but not limited to the price of our common units on future grant dates and the extent to which grants vest or are cancelled. However, we are able to estimate the number of common units that we will need to make available under the A&R Plan in order to provide us with enough common units to continue to offer competitive equity compensation through 2026. This calculation assumes that our common unit usage rate will continue to be approximately 1.5% with respect to future awards under the A&R Plan, which is consistent with the rate at which equity awards were granted and cancelled over the past three fiscal years. This usage rate is below the average equity plan share usage rate of our peer group.
If our unitholders do not approve the proposed unit increase and the other revisions in the A&R Plan, we believe we will not be able to continue to offer equity packages to our current employees and new employees, and thus may be less competitive than other companies that offer equity, in 2022 and future years. We believe that this could impede our plans for growth and impair our ability to operate our business. In addition, if we are unable to grant competitive equity awards, we may be required to offer additional cash-based incentives to replace equity as a means of competing for talent, which we believe could adversely affect our quarterly results of operations and balance sheet.
The A&R Plan is the only active equity incentive plan we currently have in place. While we could increase cash compensation to a limited extent if we are unable to grant equity incentives, we anticipate that we will have difficulty attracting, retaining and motivating our employees, directors and consultants if we are unable to issue equity grants to them. We also believe that equity grants are a more effective compensation vehicle than strictly cash, because they better align the financial interests of our employees with the interests of our unitholders, and promote actions that result in long-term unitholder value creation.
If our unitholders do not approve this LTIP Proposal, the Current Plan will continue in its current form. If this LTIP Proposal receives requisite unitholder approval, we intend to register the common units available for issuance under the A&R Plan on a registration statement on Form S-8 under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended, as soon as reasonably practicable following receipt of approval.
Description of the A&R Plan
The description of the A&R Plan set forth below is a summary of the material features of the A&R Plan. This summary, however, does not purport to be a complete description of all the provisions of the A&R Plan. This summary is qualified in its entirety by reference to the A&R Plan, which is attached to this Proxy Statement as Exhibit A.
Purpose
The purpose of the A&R Plan is to provide a means to attract and retain individuals who are essential to our growth and profitability and to encourage them to devote their best efforts to advancing our business by affording such individuals a means to acquire and maintain ownership of awards, the value of which is tied to the performance of our common units. We expect that the A&R Plan will provide for the grant of
 
8
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
common units, unit options, unit appreciation rights, restricted units, phantom units, substitute awards, and distribution equivalent rights (collectively, “awards”). These awards are intended to align the interests of employees, officers, consultants, and directors with those of our unitholders, and to give such individuals the opportunity to share in our long-term performance. Any awards that are made under the A&R Plan will be approved by the Board or the Conflicts and Compensation Committee of the Board (the “Committee”).
Key Terms of the A&R Plan
Eligibility
All employees, consultants and non-employee directors of our General Partner, Kimbell Operating and their respective affiliates providing services to the Partnership and its subsidiaries are eligible to receive awards under the A&R Plan. As of March 28, 2022, Kimbell Operating and their respective affiliates had approximately 27 employees, zero consultants, and six non-employee directors eligible to participate in the A&R Plan.
Administration
The Board has appointed the Committee to administer the A&R Plan. The Committee administers the A&R Plan pursuant to its terms and all applicable state, federal or other rules or laws. The Committee has the power to determine to whom and when awards will be granted, determine the magnitude of awards to be granted (measured in cash or in our common units), prescribe and interpret the terms and provisions of each award agreement (the terms of which may vary), accelerate the vesting provisions associated with an award, delegate duties under the A&R Plan to certain subcommittees of the Board and to our Chief Executive Officer in certain circumstances, and execute all other responsibilities permitted or required under the A&R Plan. The Committee is comprised of “non-employee directors” within the meaning of Rule 16b-3 under the Exchange Act.
Securities to be Offered
The maximum aggregate number of common units that may be issued pursuant to any and all awards under the A&R Plan will not exceed 8,241,600 common units (which includes the 4,541,600 common units previously reserved under the Current Plan and the 3,700,000 new common units to be reserved under the A&R Plan), subject to adjustment due to recapitalization or reorganization, or related to forfeitures or expiration of awards, as provided under the A&R Plan. Under the A&R Plan, the maximum aggregate grant date fair value of awards granted to a non-employee director member of the Board, in such individual’s capacity as a non-employee director, during any calendar year will not exceed $500,000 (or $600,000 in the first year in which an individual becomes a non-employee directors). As of March 31, 2022, the closing price of common units on the NYSE was $16.26.
If any common units subject to any award are not issued or transferred or cease to be issuable or transferable because the award expires, is cancelled, exercised, paid, or otherwise terminates without the delivery of common units to the award holder, those common units will again be available for issue, transfer or exercise pursuant to awards under the A&R Plan, to the extent allowable by law. However, notwithstanding the foregoing, any common units that (i) are withheld or surrendered to satisfy payment of withholding taxes, (ii) withheld or satisfied to satisfy the exercise or purchase price relating to an award, (iii) were subject to a unit-settled unit appreciation right, but were not issued upon the settlement of such unit appreciation right, or (iv) purchased or acquired in the open market upon exercise of unit option will not, in any case, be available for future issuances of awards under the A&R Plan.
Awards
Unit Options
We may grant unit options to eligible persons. Unit options are rights to acquire common units at a specified price, called the “exercise price.” The exercise price of each unit option granted under the A&R Plan will be stated in the unit option agreement and may vary; provided, however, that, the exercise price for a unit option must not be less than 100% of the fair market value per common unit as of the date of grant
 
9
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
of the unit option. Unit options may be exercised in the manner and at such times as the Committee determines for each unit option and the term of the unit option will not exceed ten years. The Committee will determine the methods and form of payment for the exercise price of a unit option and the methods and forms in which common units will be delivered to a participant. Unit options may be issued alone or in tandem with an award of distribution equivalent rights, in the discretion of the Committee.
Unit Appreciation Rights
A unit appreciation right is the right to receive, in cash or in common units, as determined by the Committee, an amount equal to the excess of the fair market value of one common unit on the date of exercise over the grant price of the unit appreciation right. The Committee will be able to make grants of unit appreciation rights and will determine the time or times at which a unit appreciation right may be exercised in whole or in part. The exercise price of each unit appreciation right granted under the A&R Plan will be stated in the unit appreciation right agreement and may vary; provided, however, that, the exercise price must not be less than 100% of the fair market value per common unit as of the date of grant of the unit appreciation right. The term of the unit appreciation right will not exceed ten years.
Restricted Units
A restricted unit is a grant of a common unit subject to a risk of forfeiture, performance conditions, restrictions on transferability and any other restrictions imposed by the Committee in its discretion. Restrictions may lapse at such times and under such circumstances as determined by the Committee. In general, each restricted unit will be entitled to receive distributions in the same manner as other outstanding common units. However, if determined by the Committee, distributions payable with respect to an award of restricted units may be subjected to the same risk of forfeiture, performance conditions, or other restrictions applicable to the restricted unit award, and such distributions will not be made with respect to such award unless and until the award vests or is forfeited, at which time, such distributions will be paid or forfeited (as applicable).
Unit Awards
The Committee will be authorized to grant common units that are not subject to restrictions. The Committee may grant common unit awards to any eligible person in such amounts as the Committee, in its sole discretion, may select.
Phantom Units
Phantom units are rights to receive common units, cash, or a combination of both at the end of a specified period, and may be granted alone or together with an award of distribution equivalent rights. The Committee may subject phantom units to restrictions (which may include a risk of forfeiture) to be specified in the phantom unit agreement that may lapse at such times determined by the Committee. Phantom units may be satisfied by delivery of common units, cash equal to the fair market value of the specified number of common units covered by the phantom unit or any combination thereof determined by the Committee. Cash distribution equivalents may be paid during or after the vesting period with respect to a phantom unit, as determined by the Committee.
Distribution Equivalent Rights
The Committee will be able to grant distribution equivalent rights in tandem with awards under the A&R Plan (other than common unit awards or an award of restricted units), or distribution equivalent rights may be granted alone. Distribution equivalent rights entitle the participant to receive cash equal to the amount of any cash distributions made by us during the period the distribution equivalent right is outstanding. Payment of cash distributions pursuant to a distribution equivalent right issued in connection with another award may be subject to the same vesting terms as the award to which it relates or different vesting terms, in the discretion of the Committee. Distribution equivalent rights may be paid directly to the participant, or may be credited to a bookkeeping account, with or without interest (at the Committee’s discretion).
 
10
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
Substitute Awards
Substitute awards may be granted under the A&R Plan in assumption of, or substitution for, awards previously granted by the Partnership or by an entity that is acquired by the Partnership or one of its affiliates, or with which the Partnership or one of its affiliates combines or merges. Any substitute awards that are unit options or unit appreciation rights may have exercises prices that are less than the fair market value of a common unit on the date of the assumption or substation if such assumption or substitution complies with Section 409A of the Code and all other applicable laws and exchange rules. Common units underlying substitute awards will not count against the pool of common units authorized for issuance under the A&R Plan.
Minimum Vesting Condition
In general, awards granted under the A&R Plan will be permitted to vest no earlier than the first anniversary of the applicable grant date. However, the following awards will be exempt from this requirement: (i) awards granted to non-employee directors that vest on the one year anniversary of the date of grant or on the date of an earlier annual meeting, so long as such annual meeting is least 50 weeks after the immediately preceding year’s annual meeting, (ii) common units delivered in lieu of fully vested cash-based awards, (iii) substitute awards, and (iv) other awards covering an aggregate number of common units that does not exceed five percent (5%) of the available unit reserve authorized for issuance under the A&R Plan. In addition, the foregoing minimum vesting rule is not intended, and will not, restrict the Committee’s ability to accelerate the exercisability or vesting of any award pursuant to its authority under the A&R Plan.
Although the Current Plan does not contain this or any other “minimum vesting” requirement applicable to all awards, in practice, the Company has historically granted awards in the form of restricted units vesting over a three-year period.
Miscellaneous
Tax Withholding
At our discretion, and subject to conditions that the Committee may impose, the payment of any applicable taxes with respect to an award may be satisfied by withholding from any payment related to an award or by the withholding of common units issuable pursuant to the award based on the fair market value of our common units, in each case, up to the maximum statutory rate.
Anti-Dilution Adjustments
In the event that any distribution, recapitalization, split, reverse split, reorganization, merger, consolidation, split-up, spin-off, combination, repurchase or exchange of our common units, issuance of warrants or other rights to purchase our common units or other similar transaction or event affects our common units, then a corresponding and proportionate adjustment will be made in accordance with the terms of the A&R Plan, as appropriate, with respect to the maximum number of units available under the A&R Plan, the number of units that may be acquired with respect to an award, and, if applicable, the exercise price of an award, in order to prevent dilution or enlargement of awards as a result of such events.
Change of Control
The effect, if any, of a change of control on outstanding awards will be described in the applicable award agreement. Pursuant to the restricted unit awards that have been granted to date pursuant to the Current Plan, if the participant remains in continuous employment or service with the Partnership and its subsidiaries from the date on which the award is granted through the date of a Change in Control, any unvested restricted units will become vested as of the date of such Change in Control. For this purpose, “Change in Control” means, and will be deemed to have occurred upon the occurrence of, one or more of the following events: (i) any “person” or “group” within the meaning of those terms as used in Sections 13(d) and 14(d)(2) of the Exchange Act, other than Kimbell GP Holdings, LLC or their respective Affiliates (as defined in the Current Plan), will become the beneficial owner, by way of merger, consolidation, recapitalization, reorganization or otherwise, of 50% or more of the combined voting power of the equity
 
11
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
interests in the Partnership or the General Partner; (ii) the limited partners of the Partnership approve, in one or a series of transactions, a plan of complete liquidation of the Partnership; (iii) the sale or other disposition by either the Partnership or the General Partner of all or substantially all of its assets in one or more transactions to any Person (as defined in the Current Plan) other than the General Partner or an Affiliate of the General Partner; or (iv) a transaction resulting in a Person other than the General Partner or an Affiliate of the General Partner being the general partner of the Partnership.
Termination of Employment or Service
The consequences of the termination of a participant’s employment, consulting arrangement or membership on the Board will be determined by the Committee in the terms of the relevant award agreement.
Restrictions on Transferability
In general, the awards granted under the A&R Plan are not transferable and may be exercised solely by a during his or her lifetime or after his or her death by the person or persons to whom his or her rights will pass by will or the laws of descent and distribution. However, in limited circumstances, if permitted by the Committee in an award agreement, certain awards may be transferred by a participant to his or her immediate family members, related family trusts, limited partnerships, or similar entities, in each case, on terms established by the Committee. Any attempt to transfer, assign, pledge or otherwise dispose of, or to subject to execution, attachment or similar process, any award contrary to the provisions set forth in the A&R Plan will be void and ineffective and will give no right to the purported transferee.
Plan Amendment and Termination
Except as required by law or the NYSE listing rules, the Board may amend, alter, suspend, discontinue, or terminate the A&R Plan in any manner (including increasing the number of common units available for issuance thereunder) without the prior consent of any participant or any other holder or beneficiary of any award.
Unless sooner terminated as provided therein, the A&R Plan shall terminate ten years from the of approval by our limited partners, or, if earlier, the date upon which common units are no longer available for issuance under the A&R Plan. The termination of the A&R Plan shall not adversely affect any awards granted prior to termination.
U.S. Federal Income Tax Consequences
The following is a summary of the general rules of current U.S. federal income tax law relating to the tax treatment of an award that may be issued under the A&R Plan. The discussion is general in nature and does not take into account a number of considerations which may apply in light of the particular circumstances of a participant. This summary is not complete and does not attempt to describe any tax consequences arising in the context of the participant’s death or the income tax laws of any local, state or foreign country in which the participant’s income or gain may be taxable.
Restricted units.   A participant generally recognizes no taxable income at the time of an award of restricted units. A participant may, however, make an election under Section 83(b) of the Code to have the grant taxed as compensation income at the date of receipt, with the result that any future appreciation or depreciation in the value of the common units granted may be taxed as capital gain or loss on a subsequent sale of the common units. If the participant does not make a Section 83(b) election, the grant will be taxed as compensation income at the full fair market value on the date the restrictions imposed on the common units expire. Unless a participant makes a Section 83(b) election, any distributions paid to the participant on the restricted units will generally be compensation income to the participant and deductible by us as compensation expense. The Company will generally be entitled to a deduction for U.S. federal income tax purposes that corresponds to the compensation income recognized by the participant. To the extent a participant realizes capital gains, as described above, we will not be entitled to any deduction for U.S. federal income tax purposes.
Phantom units.   A participant who is granted phantom units will recognize no income upon grant of the phantom units. At the time the underlying common units (or cash in lieu thereof) are delivered to a
 
12
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
participant, the participant will recognize compensation income equal to the full fair market value of the common units received. The Company will generally be entitled to a deduction for U.S. federal income tax purposes that corresponds to the compensation income recognized by the participant.
Unit options; unit appreciation rights. So long as the recipient receives the award for providing services directly to us or a subsidiary, no U.S. federal income tax is imposed on the recipient upon the grant or vesting of a unit option or unit appreciation right under the A&R Plan. Generally, upon the exercise of such unit option or unit appreciation right, the participant will be treated as receiving compensation taxable as ordinary income in the year of exercise equal to, in the case of a unit option, the excess of the fair market value of the common units on the date of exercise over the option price paid for the common units, and in the case of a unit appreciation right, the amount of cash or the value of the common units received upon exercise.
Common unit awards; distribution equivalent rights.   A participant will generally recognize ordinary income upon his or her receipt of an award of common units and of distribution equivalent rights, and the Company will generally be entitled to a deduction for U.S. federal income tax purposes that corresponds to the compensation income recognized by the participant.
Deductibility; excise taxes
Code Section 162(m).   Section 162(m) of the Code limits the federal income tax deductions a publicly held company can claim for compensation in excess of $1,000,000 paid to its Chief Executive Officer, Chief Financial Officer, any employee who is one of the top three highest compensated executive officers for the tax year, or for any employee who was a “covered employee” for any preceding taxable year beginning after December 31, 2016. Following the enactment of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, beginning with the 2018 calendar year, the $1,000,000 annual deduction limitation under Section 162(m) of the Code applies to all compensation paid to these executive officers with no exception for performance-based compensation. Accordingly, our ability to actually receive a corresponding deduction for the equity awards described above, may be limited by this new tax law.
Code Sections 280G and 4999.   The value of accelerated vesting or exercisability of an award upon the occurrence of a change in control may constitute “excess parachute payments” within the meaning of Section 280G of the Code with respect to certain participants. An amount is considered to be an “parachute payment” when a participant who constitutes a “disqualified individual” ​(as defined in Section 280G of the Code and the Treasury Regulations promulgated thereunder) with respect to the Company and its subsidiaries receives payments contingent upon a change in control (such as the accelerated vesting or exercisability of equity incentive awards) that exceed three times his or her “base amount.” The term “base amount” generally means the average annual compensation paid to such employee during the five calendar years preceding calendar year in which the change in control occurs. An “excess parachute payment” is the excess of all parachute payments made to the employee on account of a change in control over the employee’s base amount. If any amount received by an employee is characterized as an excess parachute payment, the employee is subject to a 20% excise tax on the amount of the excess pursuant to Section 4999 of the Code, and the Company is denied a tax deduction with respect to such excess.
Code Section 409A.   Section 409A of the Code generally provides that any deferred compensation arrangement must satisfy specific requirements, both in operation and in form, regarding (i) the timing of payment, (ii) the advance election of deferrals, and (iii) restrictions on the acceleration of payment. Failure to comply with Section 409A of the Code may result in the early taxation (plus interest) to the participant of deferred compensation and the imposition of a 20% penalty on the participant on such deferred amounts included in the participant’s taxable income. The Company intends to structure awards under the A&R Plan in a manner that is designed to be exempt from or comply with Section 409A of the Code, as appropriate.
Future Benefits
The awards, if any, that will be made to eligible participants under the A&R Plan depend on future decisions to be made by the Committee in its sole discretion, subject to applicable provisions of the A&R Plan. No awards have been granted that are contingent on the approval of the A&R Plan. Therefore, it is not possible to determine the benefits that will be received in the future by participants in the A&R Plan or the
 
13
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
benefits that would have been received by such participants if the A&R Plan had been in effect in the year ended December 31, 2021.

Interest of Directors and Executive Officers in the A&R Plan
Our executive officers and employees of affiliates of our General Partner who perform services for or on our behalf, and the members of the Board will be eligible to receive awards under the A&R Plan if it is approved. Accordingly, such individuals have a substantial interest in the approval of the LTIP Proposal.
Vote Required
The LTIP Proposal requires the affirmative vote of a majority of our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class.
Recommendation
OUR BOARD UNANIMOUSLY RECOMMENDS A VOTE “FOR” THE APPROVAL OF THE LTIP PROPOSAL.
 
14
TABLE OF CONTENTS​
 
PROPOSAL TWO:
ADOPTION OF THE AMENDED & RESTATED PARTNERSHIP AGREEMENT
Our Board has approved the amendment and restatement of our Third Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership, dated as of September 20, 2018, subject to the approval of our unitholders. The following is a brief summary of the amendments included in the Fourth Amended and Restated Agreement of Limited Partnership (“A&R Partnership Agreement”) (capitalized terms used but not defined herein have the meanings ascribed thereto in the A&R Partnership Agreement Partnership Agreement). This summary is qualified in its entirety by reference to the A&R Partnership Agreement, a copy of which is attached to this proxy statement as Exhibit B. A copy of the proposed A&R Partnership Agreement marked to show changes against the current partnership agreement is attached as Exhibit C. The following summary is not a substitute for reviewing the A&R Partnership Agreement and you should read this summary in conjunction with that document.
Summary of Key Changes
Timing of Quarterly Distributions
The primary change reflected in the A&R Partnership Agreement as compared to the current partnership agreement is an amendment to the timing of our quarterly distributions. The A&R Partnership Agreement does not otherwise change the Partnership’s obligation to make distributions of available cash, nor does it modify the definition of “Available Cash.”
The current partnership agreement requires us to make distributions to common unitholders within 45 days following the end of each fiscal quarter. As a result of this requirement we must declare our quarterly distribution prior to the date that we announce earnings for the particular quarter, and we must declare and pay our quarterly distributions earlier than our mineral and royalty company peers. We believe this difference in timing has led to confusion among some of our investors, and we believe that KRP would benefit by announcing its quarterly distributions on substantially the same schedule as our peers. As shown in the table below, our mineral and royalty company peers synchronize their distribution declaration with their earnings announcement. The table below compares our distribution declaration and payment dates against those of our peers:
 
15

Distribution Dates Among Public Mineral and Royalty Companies

​
The A&R Partnership Agreement will amend this requirement to permit us to announce our quarterly distribution concurrently with our earnings announcement for a particular quarter and to pay our quarterly distributions generally on the same timetable as our peers, while still retaining the requirement that we make our quarterly distributions within a designated period of time after the end of each quarter. Under the A&R Partnership Agreement, we will be required to pay our quarterly distribution no later than 60 days after the end of the first, second and third quarters of each year, and no later than 90 days after the end of the fourth quarter of each year. The A&R Partnership Agreement will require that Available Cash (as defined in the A&R Partnership Agreement) must be distributed by the “Distribution Date.” The A&R Partnership Agreement will define “Distribution Date” as:
•
with respect to the quarters ending March 31, June 30 and September 30 of each year, the earlier of: (i) 20 business days following the publication by the Partnership of results of operations with respect to such quarter (other than announcements of preliminary results of operations), or (ii) 60 calendar days following the end of such quarter; or
​
•
with respect to the quarter ending December 31 of each year, the earlier of: (i) 20 Business Days following the publication by the Partnership of results of operations with respect to such quarter (other than announcements of preliminary results of operations), or (ii) 90 calendar days following the end of such quarter.
​
Removal of Language Related to Series A Preferred Units
The A&R Partnership Agreement will remove provisions related to the Partnership’s Series A Preferred Units since they are no longer applicable following our redemption of all outstanding Series A Preferred Units in the fourth quarter of 2021. In addition, the A&R Partnership Agreement will make other non-substantive changes for clarity, including updating section references and similar changes.
 
16
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
Cash Distribution Policy and Other Matters
Except as described above, the A&R Partnership Agreement does not change any other terms of the current partnership agreement, including the Partnership’s obligation to make distributions of available cash and the definition of “Available Cash.” Please read “Item 5. Market for Registrant’s Common Equity, Related Unitholder Matters and Issuer Purchases of Equity Securities” in our Annual Report on Form 10-K for a summary of our cash distribution policy, definition of “Available Cash” and other matters related to our cash distributions.
Amendment and Restatement of Operating Company Agreement
If the adoption of the A&R Partnership Agreement is approved by our unitholders at the Special Meeting, we would expect to amend and restate the limited liability company agreement of our Operating Company to conform to the changes described above. The changes to be made to the limited liability company agreement of our Operating Company will be limited to (i) the timing of cash distributions made by our Operating Company (to align with the timing of the A&R Partnership Agreement), (ii) removal of provisions related to the Series A preferred units, and (iii) non-substantive changes for clarity, including updating section references and similar changes.
In accordance with our current partnership agreement, for purposes of approving such amendment under the terms of the current limited liability company agreement of our Operating Company, KRP will vote the Opco Units it holds in the Operating Company in the same manner as holders of common units voted, or refrained from voting, their common units on the Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal.
Vote Required
The Partnership Agreement Amendment Proposal requires the approval of our General Partner and the holders of a Unit Majority, which is a majority our outstanding common units and our outstanding Class B units, voting together as a single class.
Recommendation
OUR BOARD UNANIMOUSLY RECOMMENDS A VOTE “FOR” APPROVAL OF THE PARTNERSHIP AGREEMENT AMENDMENT PROPOSAL.
 
17
TABLE OF CONTENTS​

Fortson is Executive Vice President and Chief Investment Officer of the Kimbell Art Foundation. Pursuant to a Schedule 13D/A filed on September 21, 2021, on September 14, 2021 Kimbell Art Foundation formed a four-person Investment Committee, of which Mr. Fortson serves as Chair, with authority to manage the investments of Kimbell Art Foundation, including but not limited to the power to buy, dispose of and vote, or to direct the acquisition, disposition and voting, of investment assets owned by Kimbell Art Foundation. EQUITY COMPENSATION PLAN INFORMATION
On February 3, 2017, the Board adopted the Current Plan. On September 23, 2018, the General Partner amended the Current Plan to increase the number of common units eligible for issuance under the Current Plan by 2,500,000 common units for a total of 4,541,600 common units. EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION
Compensation Discussion and Analysis
We are providing a compensation disclosure that satisfies the requirements applicable to “emerging growth companies” as defined in the JOBS Act. Under the rules applicable to emerging growth companies, we are required to provide a Summary Compensation Table and an Outstanding Equity Awards at Fiscal Year-End Table, as well as limited narrative disclosures regarding executive compensation for our last completed fiscal year. As an emerging growth company, our Named Executive Officers, or “NEOs,” include the Chief Executive Officer and our other two most highly compensated officers. Our Named Executive Officers for the year ended December 31, 2021 include:
Name
​ ​
Principal Position
​
Robert D. Ravnaas ​ ​ Chairman and Chief Executive Officer (“CEO”) ​
R. Davis Ravnaas ​ ​ President and Chief Financial Officer (“CFO”) ​
Matthew S. Daly ​ ​ Chief Operating Officer (“COO”) ​
This discussion is intended to provide context for the tabular disclosure provided in the executive compensation tables below and to provide investors with the material information necessary to understanding our executive compensation program.
Overview of Our Executive Compensation Program
Our General Partner has sole responsibility for conducting our business and managing our operations, and its executive officers and its Board make decisions on our behalf. As is typical of publicly traded limited partnerships, we do not directly employ any of the persons responsible for managing our business. Our General Partner’s executive officers manage and operate our business as part of the services provided by Kimbell Operating Company, LLC (“Kimbell Operating”) to our General Partner under a management services agreement. All of our General Partner’s executive officers and other employees necessary to operate our business are employed and compensated by Kimbell Operating or an entity with which Kimbell Operating arranges for the provision of services. The compensation for all our executive officers is indirectly paid by us pursuant to the management services agreement with Kimbell Operating. Neither Kimbell Operating nor any affiliated entity has entered into any employment agreement with any of its executive officers.
Our General Partner’s Conflicts and Compensation Committee has adopted an annual review process for our executive compensation program. This annual review typically occurs in December of each year, and the most recent review was conducted in December 2021. This process allows us to adjust our compensation practices and targets based on prevailing market and industry conditions at the time, which aligns our compensation philosophy with our business objectives and, thereby, the interests of our executive officers with those of our unitholders.
Our Compensation Philosophy
Our compensation program is designed to reward performance and to align the interests of our executive officers with those of our unitholders. As discussed further below, we seek to tie our compensation metrics to the achievement of performance goals and the creation of unitholder value. Our long-term incentives, in the form of restricted unit awards, represent a significant portion of the total compensation paid to our executive officers. In addition, these equity awards incentivize the creation of unitholder value and encourage retention of executives and key employees through the use of multi-year vesting schedules.
Use of an Independent Compensation Advisory Firm; Peer Group and Incentive Targets
Since 2018, our General Partner’s Conflicts and Compensation Committee has engaged Pearl Meyer LLC (“Pearl Meyer”) to review our compensation practices against the norms of its competitive markets and to evaluate and recommend appropriate changes to our compensation practices consistent with our objectives.
 
23
TABLE OF CONTENTS
 
The Conflicts and Compensation Committee is responsible for approving the scope of work performed by Pearl Meyer and confirming its independence under the rules promulgated by the Dodd-Frank Act and corresponding regulations issued by the SEC and the NYSE. Pearl Meyer provides the Conflicts and Compensation Committee with an independence letter confirming its independence. As a result of its review, the Conflicts and Compensation Committee determined that Pearl Meyer was independent under the relevant rules.
Peer Group and Incentive Targets Used in Determining 2021 Compensation
In November 2018, at the direction of the Conflicts and Compensation Committee, Pearl Meyer performed a formal survey to identify a peer group of upstream oil and gas companies, as well as mineral and royalty companies, of comparable size to the Partnership. This peer group was used by the Conflicts and Compensation Committee to provide comparative market compensation data and guideposts on the basis of which the committee would determine NEO compensation for fiscal years 2019, 2020 and 2021.
In addition, in 2019 Pearl Meyer worked with management of the General Partner to assist in the development of formal measures for our long-term incentive plan (restricted unit awards) and our short-term incentive plan (cash bonus). The Conflicts and Compensation Committee approved four factors, which are described in further detail below, to be used in determining the long-term and short-term incentive awards for each of our NEOs. These four factors were used by the Conflicts and Compensation Committee in determining the amounts of cash bonus and restricted unit awards for our NEOs in 2020 and 2021. Three of the four factors were quantitative in nature and one was qualitative. The three quantitative factors consisted of achieving target objectives relating to (i) increase in barrels of oil produced (“production growth”), (ii) replacing proved developed producing reserves (“PDP reserve replacement”), and (iii) controlling cash general and administrative expense per barrel of oil equivalent (“Cash G&A expense per Boe”). The qualitative factor consisted of achieving certain core competencies, with such core competencies and the achievement thereof to be determined by the Conflicts and Compensation Committee in its discretion. The chart below displays each compensation factor, its relative weight, the target objectives and the percentage of the target cash bonuses and target restricted units to be awarded based on the level of achievement for such related target objective.

In December 2020, the Conflicts and Compensation Committee set the target cash bonuses and restricted unit awards for our NEOs for 2021. The target cash bonuses for 2021 were $575,000, $550,000 and $450,000 for each of Messrs. Robert D. Ravnaas, R. Davis Ravnaas and Matthew S. Daly, respectively. The target restricted unit awards for 2021 were 155,000, 120,000 and 85,000 restricted units for each of Messrs. Robert D. Ravnaas, R. Davis Ravnaas and Matthew S. Daly, respectively.

​ ​ ​ ​ 25% ​ ​ ​
Committee Discretion
​ ​
Above Target
​ ​
Above Target
​
The 2021 cash bonuses and restricted unit awards were calculated using the respective percentage of level of achievement of each target objective and multiplying it by the target cash bonus and restricted unit award. Our actual results achieved with respect to two of the three quantitative compensation factors were above the target objective for 2021, and our actual results for the third quantitative compensation factor met the target objective. For the qualitative compensation factor, the Conflicts and Compensation Committee determined that the NEOs had exceeded the target objective for the achievement of core competencies. As each compensation factor was equally weighted at 25% and the actual results achieved for three of the four compensation factors were above the target objective, with actual results for the fourth factor meeting the target objective, the Conflicts and Compensation Committee determined that the cash bonuses and restricted unit awards for Messrs. Robert D. Ravnaas, R. Davis Ravnaas and Matthew S. Daly would be set at amounts equal to 137.5% of their 2021 target amounts. The chart below displays the actual 2021 cash bonuses and restricted unit awards for our NEOs based on the level of achievement of each compensation factor.
